sentences.text: The intended-- so we stayed there.
And soon as there was a little, you know, daybreak, a little bit, she said, we're going.
She never thought of taking the offer.
Never.
Never even--
And the-- and the-- and the minute he-- he mentioned this, it just-- she was interested, I think, just to stay this one--
--the one night and not to be outside.
And the barn was a little warmer.
And she didn't discuss the offer with you.
I wouldn't have an idea what-- then.
Hm.
I don't know.
I'm not going to go back how old I was.
That was--
Roughly, 15, 16?
No.
I wasn't that old.
Less.
I was a teenager, let's put it this way.
And she just left, slipped down.
We went down the ladder.
And we started to walk again.
[SIGHS] First, we started to find, you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a way how to get out of that, you know, place.
Which way-- you know, north or south or east or what?
We didn't know where-- where-- where we were.
But we walked.
Somehow we found a way to get out.
And we walked.
How-- and actually, it winded up that we were not too far from Lublin.
But we didn't know the way.
It was just the sticks.
It was just--
No houses.
No.
No houses.
No fields and things like that.
Then finally, we-- we saw it's going to snow.
You know, the weather turns cold.
It was very cold and windy.
And it started to snow.
We walked about a-- most of the day.
The month was October or November.
No.
That was 1942.
So when we walked, we saw another house from faraway.
When it gets cold, you see smoke.
OK.
And when the-- the sky and the sun starts to come up, then you see smoke in the sky there.
So towards that, we walked where the smoke comes from.
And we came nearer.
And there was a house and a barn.
And we just walked in.
Near where the barn is in the house.
It was so early.
And this farmer was ready, I think, to start either going someplace or what.
He was in the barn taking the horses out.
So we walked in.
And he didn't want anybody neighbor, maybe a faraway neighbor.
There were no neighbors anyway.
Right.
He was the only one.
Into the house-- and he didn't ask any questions.
Because he understood what was going on.
Let us in.
And he said to his wife, the first thing, we have to change our clothes, what we wore.
And she gave us some peasant--
--peasant clothes, changed the whole outlook, the way we looked, and a babushka, a kerchief and everything.
And he said, I'm going to put the horses in into the buggy.
And you go into the train.
I'm going to take you to the train.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he put-- he put, you know, us into that carriage, whatever it was.
And we went.
It didn't took too long, maybe an hour.
And we got out and the train [INAUDIBLE].
No, a train.
It was going into Lublin to the city.
We went in in the train.
It was another thing, because there were a lot of people in the train.
And we-- we were scared.
So my sister just her eyes, she wrapped around her kerchief around her mouth, her nose, just, you know, that she can barely see and did the same thing.
And we were shivering just with our heads down.
Maybe somebody's going to walk by and see, you know, recognize you.
Right.
You were passing now for polish girls instead of Jewish girls.
Right.
So we went near the city.
The train stopped.
We walked out.
And then she knew where she is.
Because she knew probably the city very well.
My sister, where she is she recognize.
And then she knew somebody who was known.
She arranged for the ones that try to survive on Aryan papers.
She arranges things.
As--
As long as you have the money, like my mother said, buy yourself time.
[SIGHS] We walked.
And we walked in.
What a beautiful place that was.
You cannot imagine.
My sister knew her.
She had striked the conversation.
[SIGHS] And finally, I could breathe easy.
Because we were inside with somebody who's going to help us.
So she had a conversation.
It was less [INAUDIBLE] too long the conversation.
My sister didn't even try [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to go into Russia hands or anything.
She just said she needed some toothpaste.
She needed a toothbrush, a toothpaste.
Goes to me, she walked out.
And I've never-- And I waited an hour or two.
So she came over.
That woman came over, a very nice, you know, middle aged, aristocratic-- I mean, you know.
You could see that she had a good life.
And she said-- your-- she walked over.
She said, your sister will never come back.
I don't know what she-- what she said.
Apparently, my-- she said to my sister that her son is one of the highest ranking officers who is in charge of all the slave--
--labor they take from Europe, Poland, Europe all over, the highest ranking.
He probably is something like Himmler or Goring, whatever.
So that's what came out of her mouth.
And my sister, all she had to hear that probably.
So she never came back.
No.
She figured that in my age, I'd be probably maybe safe there, that I'm smart enough to--
--save [INAUDIBLE] to save my life.
Yeah.
To save my life, to say things.
The only thing I told her that, you know, we have nobody left.
Everybody was killed.
We are bombed out and things like that.
And that's the end of that.
So she saw-- she said that her son is coming.
That was already later in the afternoon, coming home from the office.
I was worried about that, but that's what I didn't expected.
When he came in, I saw a statue and shiny boots with medals over 6 feet tall on a Nazi.
He came in.
And the first thing he raised his arm, Heil Hitler.
I was numb.
I felt that, you know, my life ended right then and-- right here.
I said, I walked in into their hands.
Here, I tried to run away to save my life.
I walked right in.
I couldn't believe it.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then he said to her mother, you know, they went in in another room.
And she told him a [NON-ENGLISH] probably, a story what.
So he told her that he's going to register me for school and-- and things like that.
Sounds good.
But I didn't know.
She told me that, that woman.
But she knew the truth.
She knew the truth.
And she-- her life was at stake here.
Yeah.
Her life was at stake.
So it was supper time.
She set the table.
I felt like I'm burning out-- up.
I have fever.
I'm shivering here.
I have fever.
And I'm shivering.
You know, my-- my teeth were chattering.
And I just couldn't sit still or anything.
What am I going to do?
I don't know where I am.
And here I am in a trap.
He said, he looked and he saw maybe if he's going to take me to a movie.
This is going to cheer me up maybe or what.
He took me.
And before the movie, this-- it was sold out.
So we didn't.
And it was snowing outside.
It was snowing heavily already.
When I walked with him, it was right-- not far.
And I was afraid that somebody's going to see me.
So I had a scarf.
And I threw this babushka, you know, to cover my-- my face.
We walked back in.
I guess he went-- you know, he had a [INAUDIBLE].
So he went.
And I had a nice clean bed in a long time.
But I couldn't sleep.
I was shivering.
I was cold.
I had a high fever.
I don't know.
I was so sick.
In the morning, I couldn't wait for the morning.
I got up.
And she got up, that woman.
And she was probably worried a lot--
--what's going to happen.
I didn't say anything.
She was busy doing things.
I just put on wherever I had on me, you know, wore.
Because he told her to change clothes for me and everything.
I just put on.
And I walked out the door.
Sick.
I just walked.
I didn't look back.
I just walked soft, brisk.
But I didn't know which way to go.
I didn't know that was the outskirts of Lublin.
I-- I had no idea where I am, but I didn't care.
I just-- whatever is going to happen is going to happen.
So then I hit a market.
Finally, I saw a market.
I walked maybe two hours.
I saw a market selling things like food and-- and live chicken and things.
I walked over.
The first thing I took out-- I had money on me.
I had money all over, you know, that coat.
Bills, they were worthless, but you had them.
And I paid for piece of peasant bread, that black bread.
And I put this bread under my arm.
And she was yelling at me, that woman.
She said change, that she was trying to give me change.
Give change
I didn't look back.
I just run.
And then I run.
I saw trucks with prisoners jumping in-- in the stripe uniforms.
And there were dead all over the-- the-- the street.
And the German soldiers with the German shepherds, the Nazis with the German shepherds--
Whoever would try to try to run, they just tear you to pieces.
From rooftops, they were running on the rooftops hiding.
You would never believe.
And I didn't know where I am and what's going on.
And then they were trying to, you know, grab from the street.
And then I felt with a club, I was clubbed in the back twice.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I looked behind me.
And I saw this with a German shepherd.
And he said in German, [SPEAKING IN GERMAN] that I try to run away from the-- from the line.
They were lined up to go where, I had no idea.
And he pushed me in into the line.
Then I said to myself, dear God, God help me.
That was my mother, my father, prayed for me wherever they are.
I'm saved now, That I'm saved, because I'm pushed in, what I was trying to do all along.
That was the-- the thing that I was looking for.
That was a labor group of Poles of all kind to go to be shipped to Germany.
So I went in-- in that line.
And by the time they got there on the platform with the train, trains were ready.
And I walked around.
There were so many people there.
And I look down.
I never looked up.
Because I was afraid that somebody's going to see me.
I looked down.
And I remember what my sister wore.
And I said, oh my God.
Then I looked up, there she was.
All I did.
I don't say a word.
I took the piece of bread.
I broke in half, I gave her half.
And then I gave her half.
We looked at each other for a-- for a split second.
And she's went away again.
And I was here by myself.
I said, dear God--
I said, I have to grow up.
I said, I am on my own.
I had no idea why, but there-- there I am.
That--
That was-- that was at that time that, you know, she went to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when she was pregnant [INAUDIBLE] told you, nine months pregnant.
And I have a picture of her first born, little girl.
I showed you the picture?
Yeah.
[INAUDIBLE] I found it last night.
Yeah, I found it last night.
They went-- they-- they-- they died in-- in the crematorium.
They-- they put them to death.
What did I say?
Majdanek.
They went there, because they didn't want to be apart.
They want to be together.
They wanted to be together.
First, my sister went.
And I was told he worked for a while.
He was always on the medication.
And under this condition, somehow he survived.
And they burned him.
They gassed him.
I finally got onto the train.
We traveled all night [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and all day, all day.
The days were the most worst things, because you're afraid somebody might recognize you, some Pole or what.
It was a train-- that was not a bad train, because--
A passenger actually.
Not the kind of train that you would say--
Not a cattle train.
That was a regular train that took to forced-- forced labor to Germany.
So we stopped.
We traveled for a night and a day.
Finally, we stopped.
I don't remember the city where we stopped.
And there was the-- that the came from all over Germany to select slaves, you know, workers for the factories or farmers or whatever they need.
We were lined up.
And there I met my sister again.
And we lined up.
So they picked from ours, you know, our group.
And then she saw, she slipped away from that group.
I think she did a smart thing, because that group sounded to her very you know, give away.
There was some of them that still were from the gymnasium, the-- your uniforms or things.
That gives you away right away.
Who goes to-- not a peasant is gonna-- you want to go to slave labor to Germany.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That-- so she slipped away.
And I stood there.
I thought she went the other way.
And I-- then one of those German buyers, they bought people for the factories.
And there were whole group of us.
And we wind up one group, 13 of us among 40 something Polish women.
We wind up-- until we wind up there, there was a lot of things going on.
They found Jewish men.
And there was on the loud speaker to come out and see they're hanging Jewish men.
They were trying to hide out as, you know, gentiles.
They recognized them in the shower.
They were circumcised.
Couple a day everyday, they find some.
So when we finally-- I'm going to make the story short.
We wind up in a Bitterfeld in a aluminum-- aluminum work.
It was a Farben.
It was a Farben.
Farben.
IG Farben.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were about 40 of us.
We were 13 Jewish.
There was a mother and daughter and a mother, Mrs.
Felix, her daughter Kitty, and-- and Mrs.
Felix.
And there were two sisters.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Brenner-- their names were Brenner.
After, I found out that their names were Brenner.
And there was another sister.
And her sister, Hannah.
She was about eight years old.
[INAUDIBLE] daughter from near Lublin.
So and there was another.
Her name was Maria.
She was the one that actually got us into trouble, because she spoke with a-- you have to speak with no accident.
The-- the Polacks are going to recognize you, that you're Jewish when you have a accent.
OK?
And she had an accent.
You got to-- you got to speak without a accent.
You got to speak a perfect Polish in order not to be recognized.
Either you're too intelligent for them, or then you're not.
So that is a giveaway.
So she was the one who always-- and she talked, you know.
You could never-- she never shut up.
She always talked.
So this thing, when we started walking, there is from Krakow, they were from Krakow.
Her daughter worked in the office, in a doctor's office.
And there was a nurse from Warsaw, that Polish woman.
And she was the nurse.
She had an eye on this doctor.
And this doctor fell in love with a Jewish, you know.
She worked in the office.
She was the most beautiful woman that I ever saw, tall, beautiful.
That started.
Being me, you know, my age, this nurse sort of got-- she liked me.
OK?
And she told me things.
She said, there are Jews hiding out here.
And I know it.
There is specially one of them I have to get rid of.
So there was day after day I knew.
And I-- we were afraid.
Among ourselves, we knew who was Jewish.
I had a feeling that who was Jewish already, but we didn't.
We didn't talk among us, because-- It's too dangerous.
--you even afraid--
--to talk among your own.
Sometimes you go into the ladies room or what.
And you just-- I said-- I said to her [INAUDIBLE] that, you know, we in danger here.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I knew they are sending a spy.
She spoke about [INAUDIBLE] maybe 20 languages or so.
And we were called in one by one.
She asked us some tricky questions, things.
And one Friday they came in-- did I told you about where I worked there?
No.
They put me to work on a machine.
They have-- they brought aluminum in sacks, like sugar or what.
It looked.
And there were big machines like press machines, like-- like-- like printing presses, big machines.
So after, the aluminum was emptied into things that melt it.
There was a big fire.
And it melted to liquid.
Yeah.
And they put and-- and-- and that, you know, turned to aluminum.
It hardens.
So I-- my job was to take each sack-- the sacks came in-- and slide them in into a, you, know, and a clean the dust out.
Purified the sacks, and you stack the sacks up.
So my hair and my face, they gave us those blue uniform slacks, and a-- and a thing to wear.
Coat.
I was from blue, it went white from that--
--aluminum, from that powder.
My face, every time I needed to see where I put the sack, and I had to wipe to get that dust of my eyes.
One day the inspection comes in.
And he said to-- he asked that foreman, whoever it was, the German, how old we are.
And he said, I don't think-- if they're not going to get any protection and masks, for the-- at least for the mouth and nose, that they're not going to survive.
Sure.
A lot of dust and aluminum dust.
Yeah.
Not going to survive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So it then took about a week or so.
They came in with masks just to cover the nose and mouth.
But the eyes was still-- you were-- then, they gave us a kerchief, you know, for the-- for my head.
And that used to go in to take showers after work.
You couldn't get out that aluminum from your hair.
And you rinse and rinse.
And my eyes-- my vision got, you know, so blurred.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So I was OK.
If I have to survive this way, that's OK, too.
And we got paid for that.
Every week you got paid.
[LAUGHS] I don't know what you did with the money, because there was nothing to buy except potato salad and what.
That was nothing.
You get up.
It was still dark, 4:30 in the morning.
[INAUDIBLE] in they came, you know, to wake you up with the guns.
Roll call.
You get up, and you go to work.
Yeah.
She was in charge, Mrs.
Felix.
She was in charge for-- because she spoke about-- she was a professor of English.
She was a-- English.
She was brought from England to Bielsk to teach in the university.
That's how she wound up, you know, in--
OK.
Well--
Not now.
Not now.
When we-- when we continue.
OK.
